Party_Details.Name: TYC Investment Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The company was established as a family holding company to manage shares in The Hour Glass Limited and other family assets.
The company became bound by a Deed requiring both Governing Directors to jointly approve all future corporate payments via a voucher system.
The company received an invoice from KPMG for $19,424.05 regarding tax and accounting advisory services.
An extraordinary general meeting was convened where resolutions were passed to authorize the commencement of legal proceedings to resolve a management deadlock.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company can utilize shareholder reserve powers to authorize the commencement of litigation and the appointment of solicitors when its board is deadlocked.
Party_Details.Rule: Management powers are generally reserved to the board, but limited reserve powers may be implied to shareholders if the board is unable or unwilling to act and such steps are necessary to enable the company to function.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff stated that the entrenched joint-approval system paralyzed corporate action, necessitating judicial intervention via shareholder resolutions. The Defendants argued that the board's powers could not be usurped by shareholders under the company's constitution.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court dismissed the application. While it recognized a limited reserve power in shareholders to appoint solicitors during a deadlock, it ruled that shareholders could not authorize a director to unilaterally sign cheques in contravention of an entrenched joint-approval mechanism.

Party_Details.Name: Tay Yun Chwan Henry

Facts.Text: He co-founded the family holding company and served as a permanent Governing Director.
He executed an agreement introducing a strict payment voucher system requiring joint approval for all corporate fund disbursements.
His associated company, Amstay Pte Ltd, paid a $19,424.05 corporate invoice and subsequently sought reimbursement from the holding company.
He convened an extraordinary general meeting to pass resolutions enabling him to unilaterally authorize corporate payments.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director possesses the authority to unilaterally appoint legal counsel or override a joint-approval payment mechanism through majority shareholder resolutions.
Party_Details.Rule: A director acting without a valid board resolution cannot bind the company to significant management decisions if the constitution requires joint consent.
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant supported the company's efforts to break the deadlock and effect payments. The opposing director stated that his unilateral appointment of lawyers and attempt to bypass the voucher system was a contravention of the corporate constitution.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the director lacked the authority to unilaterally commence the action in the name of the company or to override the joint-approval framework established by the settlement documents.

Party_Details.Name: Chan Siew Lee Jannie

Facts.Text: She co-founded the family holding company and served as a permanent Governing Director with 44% voting rights.
She began withholding her signature on corporate payment vouchers, effectively blocking the disbursement of funds for property and professional expenses.
She refused to approve the payment of a $19,424.05 invoice from KPMG citing a lack of clarity regarding the scope of work.
She formally challenged the authority of the external solicitors appointed by her co-director and the shareholder group.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a governing director's refusal to approve specific corporate payments constitutes a contravention of management obligations when the governance structure requires joint consent.
Party_Details.Rule: A director is entitled to scrutinize corporate expenses and exercise independent judgment; a court will not compel a director's management decision unless it contravenes the law or the company's constitution.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff stated her refusal to sign cheques was obstructive and paralyzed the business. The Defendant argued her refusal was a legitimate exercise of management oversight regarding expenses she deemed questionable.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge affirmed that her refusal to sign cheques was a management function. The court declined to rewrite the parties' contract or imply terms restricting her power to withhold approval, as her conduct was already governed by existing statutory duties.
